original_abstract: While some might suggest that these two agendas are complementary—with decolonial critiques opening political space for decentring strategies to implement pragmatic, incremental change—there is no assurance that such synergy will occur. This is particularly true if the distinct goals of decentring and decoloniality continue to be blurred. While a decentring approach might improve the perceived legitimacy and efficiency of EU foreign policy, it risks leaving the underlying "coloniality of power" untouched or even further entrenched. Consequently, rather than simply attempting to "decentre Europe," we must shift toward decolonial futures that center the democratic struggle against persistent colonial structures and hierarchies.
humanized_abstract: Many would argue that the two agendas can be seen in tandem – that decolonial critical thinking creates space for decentring as a pragmatic means of achieving evolutionary change. But there is no guarantee that these two different logics will reinforce each other. And there is a real risk that they will continue to be confused with each other, with decentring being promoted as a way of increasing the EU’s legitimacy and effectiveness as a foreign policy actor – which would amount to leaving the coloniality of power intact, or even reinforcing it. Rather than continue to talk of ‘decentring Europe’ we need to start talking about decolonial futures that serve the goals of the democratic struggle against colonial structures and relations of inequality.
